title: Intro Algorithm Analysis Quiz (Thorough)
instructions: Answer all questions. MCQs are auto-graded. Short answers are graded by Claude by default (or self mode).

questions.prompt: Which best matches the course framing of an algorithm?
options: A step-by-step method for getting from problem to solution.
A hardware architecture for faster execution.
Any mathematical formula with one variable.

questions.prompt: In the intro slides, a problem is best described as:
options: A syntax error in source code.
A question to which we seek an answer.

questions.prompt: Which is a valid representation form for algorithms highlighted in the slides?
For sequential search over $n$ elements, the best-case time complexity shown is:
For adding all array members, the slide table indicates best/average/worst are all:
For exchange sort in the table, best/average/worst are all:
Binary search is efficient mainly because it:
options: Divides the candidate region in half repeatedly.
Uses recursion only for balanced arrays.

questions.prompt: In analysis, $T(n)$ is defined as:
options: The wall-clock time in milliseconds on one specific machine.
Number of times the basic operation executes for input size $n$.
Number of lines of source code.
Number of variables used in the algorithm.

questions.prompt: Computational complexity in the slides primarily refers to the amount of:
options: Resources needed to execute an algorithm.

questions.prompt: Which pair is listed under computational complexities?
Asymptotic analysis in the intro deck emphasizes, unless specified otherwise:
Which statement best distinguishes pseudocode from source code?
options: Pseudocode is compiled and executable; source code is not.
Pseudocode is human-readable high-level description; source code is executable program text.
They are identical except for comments.
Source code is more abstract than pseudocode.

questions.prompt: Which is true about algorithms solving the same problem (from algorithm characteristics)?
options: They must use the same idea and have similar speed.
Only one valid algorithm can exist for a problem.
Different algorithms can use very different ideas and run at dramatically different speeds.
Efficiency differences are only due to hardware.

questions.prompt: Which cost model assigns operation cost proportional to number of bits involved?
The slides compare two GCD approaches: consecutive integer checking and Euclid's algorithm. Explain in 3-5 sentences why Euclid's method is generally more efficient.
questions.expected: Consecutive checking may test many candidate divisors one by one from $\min(m,n)$ downward. Euclid's algorithm uses modulo and reduces problem size quickly each iteration. This usually requires far fewer steps for large inputs while still producing the same $\gcd$.

questions.prompt: Using the Euclid recurrence $\gcd(n, m) = \gcd(m, n \bmod m)$, compute $\gcd(84, 30)$. Show the remainder chain and final answer.
For sequential search over an array of size $n$, state best-case, average-case, and worst-case time, and briefly justify each.
questions.expected: Best: $O(1)$ when first element matches. Average: $O(n)$ because expected checks grow linearly with $n$. Worst: $O(n)$ when target is absent or at last position.

questions.prompt: Give one clear example each of algorithm (abstract), pseudocode (structured human-readable), and source code (language-specific executable) using the same simple problem.
questions.expected: A valid response should show the same problem at three abstraction levels: high-level algorithm steps, pseudocode with control structures, and concrete code in a language (for example Python/C).

questions.prompt: In one paragraph, explain the role of input size $n$, basic operation, and $T(n)$ in algorithm analysis.
questions.expected: $n$ quantifies instance size; the basic operation defines the unit of work to count; $T(n)$ is the count of that operation as a function of $n$. Together they provide machine-independent growth analysis of efficiency.

questions.prompt: The slides list motivations for analysis (system performance, time-sensitive apps, limited-space apps). Pick one real-world app scenario and explain how choosing a better algorithm materially changes outcomes.
questions.expected: A correct response must tie one scenario (for example search latency, routing, mobile memory limits) to measurable impact from better complexity (faster response, lower memory, lower cost, better scalability).
